coredata.dc:title: Preparation and performance of carbon dot decorated copper sulphide/carbon nanotubes hybrid composite as supercapacitor electrode materials
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Tingkai
Peng, Xiarong
Zhao, Xin
Hu, Jingtian
Jiang, Tao
Lu, Xiaofeng
Zhang, Heng
Li, Tiehu
Ahmad, Ishaq
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon dot decorated copper sulphide/carbon nanotubes (CuS@CD-CNTs) composite with a unique three-dimensional (3D) grape string-like structure was directly prepared via in situ hydrothermal process at the temperature of 180 °C for 12 h and applied for supercapacitors. The chemical composition and morphology were systematically tested by XRD, Raman, SEM and TEM characterization techniques. The as-prepared CuS@CD-CNTs composite brings out a novel 3D grape string-like structure, where the CuS spheres are distributed homogeneously with CNTs as a result of the addition of carbon dot. As the active material of pseudocapacitor electrode, the CuS@CD-CNTs composite delivers superior electrochemical properties with a decent specific capacitance of 736.1 F g−1 at the current density of 1 A g−1. Also, the CuS@CD-CNTs composite showed exceptional cycling stability, maintaining 92% retention after 5000 charge-discharge cycles. Such superior electrochemical properties owe to the collective contribution of CuS, CD and CNTs and 3D grape string-like architecture. The excellent electrochemical results above suggest that CuS@CD-CNTs composite has promising electrochemical energy storage application in supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper sulphide
Carbon dot
3D grape string-like structure
Electrode material

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dot decorated copper sulphide/carbon nanotubes composite (CuS@CD-CNTs)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51672221 , 51872231 ), the Key Industrial Chain Project of Shaanxi Province ( 2018ZDCXL-GY-08-07 ), the Seed Foundation of Innovation and Creation for Graduate Students in Northwestern Polytechnical University ( ZZ2018006 ) and National College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program ( 201810699094 ). We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Northwestern Polytechnical University for SEM and TEM testing. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the preparation process of CuS@CD-CNTs composite.
(a) XRD patterns of CuS, CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs, (b) Raman spectra of CNTs, CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs
SEM images of (a) CuS, (b) CuS-CNTs, (c,d) CuS@CD-CNTs and its EDS elemental mapping of (e) copper, (f) sulfur and (g) carbon images.
(a.b) TEM images of CuS@CD-CNTs, (c.d) HRTEM images of CD and CuS@CD-CNTs, respectively.

(a–c) CV curves of CuS, CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs respectively, (d) Comparison of CV curves of CuS, CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs, (e) The capacitance retention curves of CuS, CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs, (f) The GCD curves of CuS@CD-CNTs
(a) The specific capacitances of CuS@CD-CNTs at different current densities, (b) Nyquist plots.
(a) Cyclic performance of CuS@CD-CNTs, the inset shows charge-discharge curves.

head.ce:title: Preparation and performance of carbon dot decorated copper sulphide/carbon nanotubes hybrid composite as supercapacitor electrode materials

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: NPU-NCP Joint International Research Center on Advanced Nanomaterials and Defects Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: NPU-NCP Joint International Research Center on Advanced Nanomaterials and Defects Engineering, Experimental Physics Department, National Center for Physics, Islamabad, 44000, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon dot decorated copper sulphide/carbon nanotubes (CuS@CD-CNTs) composite with a unique three-dimensional (3D) grape string-like structure was directly prepared via in situ hydrothermal process at the temperature of 180 °C for 12 h and applied for supercapacitors. The chemical composition and morphology were systematically tested by XRD, Raman, SEM and TEM characterization techniques. The as-prepared CuS@CD-CNTs composite brings out a novel 3D grape string-like structure, where the CuS spheres are distributed homogeneously with CNTs as a result of the addition of carbon dot. As the active material of pseudocapacitor electrode, the CuS@CD-CNTs composite delivers superior electrochemical properties with a decent specific capacitance of 736.1 F g−1 at the current density of 1 A g−1. Also, the CuS@CD-CNTs composite showed exceptional cycling stability, maintaining 92% retention after 5000 charge-discharge cycles. Such superior electrochemical properties owe to the collective contribution of CuS, CD and CNTs and 3D grape string-like architecture. The excellent electrochemical results above suggest that CuS@CD-CNTs composite has promising electrochemical energy storage application in supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: The massive consumption of fossil fuels and the deteriorating environmental problems are getting more and more seriously and energy sources of high efficiency and sustainability are desperately in need, especially new technologies in relation to the energy conversion and storage. Supercapacitor refers to novel energy storage device, which has the features of high-speed charging and discharging of capacitors and the energy storage characteristics of the battery [1]. Based on the dramatic advantages of short charging time, high power density and excellent cycling stability, the application of supercapacitors have captured more and more attention. Transition metal oxides and conducting polymers are generally utilized for supercapacitors along with pseudocapacitive reaction [2]. Currently, transition metal chalcogenides such as ZnS [3], MnS [4], CoS [5], MoS2 [6], NiS [7] and NiCo2O4 [8] are served as potential pseudocapacitive supercapacitors. Among the numerous transition metal sulfides, CuS is a typical semiconductor material which is inexpensive, abundant and environment friendly. Extensive investigations have been published, such as, Guo et al. [9] firstly synthesized double-shell CuS nanocages. Huang et al. [10] reported that various morphologies CuS nanosheets were obtained using two kinds of surfactants. Xu et al. [11] prepared CuS nanosheets by dealloying a Ti–Cu amorphous alloy, exhibiting high specific capacitance of 276 F g−1 at 5 mV s−1. However, it is unsatisfied to be utilized as electrode material because of the drawbacks concerning poor cyclic stability and low electrical conductivity. Therefore, researchers pay more attention to the study of hybrid EDLC/pseudocapacitor electrode materials [12]. With the development of transition metal sulfides for supercapacitors, hybrid supercapacitors based on CuS have emerged as the times require [13].

In the other hand, carbon materials are deemed to be potential electrode materials owing to their abundant resources, low expense and large specific surface area [14,15]. The commonly used carbon materials are graphene [16,17], carbon nanotube [18], carbon fiber [19], activated carbon [20], etc. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are nano-tubular materials made of single or multiple layers graphite carbon curled together, which possess large specific surface area, excellent conductivity and excellent cyclic stability. Therefore, synthesizing the composite of CuS and CNTs can give full play to the virtues of every component, enhancing the specific capacitance and cyclic stability simultaneously. Carbon dot (CD) is a new carbon material with size below 10 nm and consists of a nanocrystalline core with major sp2-hybridized carbon and an amorphous shell containing abundant surface functional groups like hydroxyl, carboxylate, carbonyl on the surface. As a group of low toxicity, good dispersibility, simple preparation and biocompatibility, CD has been widely researched in diverse fields such as bioimaging [21], photocatalysis [22], sensors [23] and electrocatalysis [24]. Recently, several studies into supercapacitors have been reported, showing bright prospect of CD in supercapacitors applications. Lv et al. [25] synthesized carbon dot-induced MnO2 nanowire utilized as supercapacitor electrode materials. Zhang et al. [26] designed graphene oxide/carbon dots/polypyrrole composite, exhibiting high specific capacitance of 576 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1. CD in composites can not only improve conductivity because of the edge effects and quantum confinement, but also promote the redox reaction, enhancing electrochemical performance.

In this work, carbon dot decorated copper sulphide/carbon nanotubes composite (CuS@CD-CNTs) was synthesized successfully via a simple hydrothermal method. Here, CD acts out as a good interacting agent between CuS and CNTs because of the large number of superficial functional group. The electrochemical properties improved due to the unique 3D grape string-like structure and the synergistic effect of CuS, CNTs and CD, including high specific capacitance and excellent cyclic stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dot decorated copper sulphide/carbon nanotubes composite (CuS@CD-CNTs)
ce:section.ce:para: The MWCNTs (purity > 95%) were dissolved in a mixed solution of concentrated sulfuric acid and nitric acid (VH2SO4:VHNO3 = 3:1) and ultrasound treated for 4 h to remove the catalysts, improving their purity and activity. The CuS@CD-CNTs was fabricated via a simple hydrothermal reaction, shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, 20 mg acid CNTs were dispersed in 30 mL distilled water by sonication for 1 h. Then 100 mg copper sulphate (CuSO4·5H2O) and 99.4 mg sodium thiosulphate (Na2S2O3·5H2O) were dissolved in 30 mL distilled water and 360 mg of glucose was dissolved in 30 mL distilled water with 35 mg of ascorbic acid. Subsequently, the solutions were mixed uniformly under constant magnetic stirring for 1 h. The obtained mixture was put into a 100 mL teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C from room temperature. The CuS@CD-CNTs composite was obtained after the hydrothermal preparation of 12 h. Finally, the sample was washed repeatedly by distilled water to PH = 7 and dried at 60 °C overnight. The pure CuS and CuS-CNTs were synthesized by the same method to compare.

The compositions of CuS, CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs composite were operated on X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, X’Pert PRO, Cu-Kα radiation) in 10–80°. The morphologies of the samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Verios G4, FEI) at 30 kV and HRTEM analysis was tested using a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai F30 G2, FEI) at 300 kV. The Raman spectra were investigated using Raman spectrometer (Raman, Alpha 300R, Germany).

To evaluate the electrochemical performance of samples, cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanometric charge-discharge (GCD) and electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were conducted in three-electrode system of CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Company, China) in 3.0 M KOH electrolyte solution. The platinum foil and saturated calomel electrode were used as the auxiliary and reference electrodes respectively and the working electrode was prepared from mixed slurry containing CuS@CD-CNTs, PVDF and carbon black at a mass ratio of 80: 10: 10. The CV curves were tested in 0–0.5 V from 10 to 100 mV s−1 and the GCD curves were tested in 0–0.5 V from 1 to 7 A g−1. The EIS measurements were conducted in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz–100 kHz at an open circuit potential with an ac perturbation of 5 mV.

To analyse the composition of samples, the XRD patterns of CuS, CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs were measured and presented in Fig. 2 (a). The peaks marked at 2θ = 27.681°, 29.277°, 31.784°, 32.852°, 38.835°, 47.941°, 52.714° and 59.345° are in line with the d(101), d(102), d(103), d(006), d(105), d(110), d(108) and d(116) planes of CuS (JCPDS No. 06–0464), which confirm the formation of CuS. Moreover, all the characteristic diffraction peaks also appear in CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs, indicating the good crystallinity of CuS in the two samples. The characteristic peak at 2θ = 26° is representative of (002) plane of carbon materials. The peak emerging at 2θ = 26° proves the existence of carbon nanotubes in CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs composite. The intensity of diffraction peak at 2θ = 26° decreases and it can be a result of the addition of carbon dot with small crystalline grain size in CuS@CD-CNTs composite. The crystalline size is calculated from the full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the most intense diffraction peak (103) using Debye-Scherrer formula and the calculated particle size is 32.2 nm. So it is possible that the CuS spheres originate from the arrangements of nanoparticles. The Raman spectra of CNTs, CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs were shown in Fig. 2(b). The D band is relevant to structural disorder and defects and G band results from the 1st order scattering of the E2g phonon of sp2 carbon atoms. Consequently, the numerical value of ID/IG is generally served to assess the disorder extent of carbon material. A sharp peak appears at the Raman shift of 474 cm−1 in the Raman spectra of CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs, representing the presence of CuS. The intensity ratio of CuS-CNTs (ID/IG = 1.03) and CuS@CD-CNTs (ID/IG = 0.93) are higher than that of CNTs (ID/IG = 0.91), revealing more disorders and defects in the reaction process.

The structures and morphologies of CuS, CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs were investigated by SEM and TEM technologies. The CuS spheres are formed with wrinkled surface in a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor. Obviously, the diameter of CuS spheres are at the range of 1–2 μm in Fig. 3 (a). In Fig. 3(b), the CuS spheres and CNTs are separated and agglomerated seriously in CuS-CNTs composite. Fig. 3(c and d) shows the SEM images of CuS@CD-CNTs composite at different magnification. The CuS spheres interact with CNTs intimately and distribute homogeneously in the CuS@CD-CNTs composite as a result of the addition of CD, which speed up the electrons and ions diffusion greatly and improved the electrochemical performance. Simultaneously, the diameter of CuS spheres declines to the range of 100–500 nm. As shown in Fig. 3(e–g), the EDS elemental mapping was performed and copper, sulfur and carbon elements are detected in the CuS@CD-CNTs composite, illustrating the existence and even distribution of copper, sulfur and carbon elements.

Furthermore, TEM analysis was tested to analyse the internal structure of CuS@CD-CNTs. The crosslinked interconnected 3D structure of CuS@CD-CNTs composite is presented in the TEM images of Fig. 4 . (a,b). The bending carbon nanotubes tangle between CuS spheres homogeneously and the diameter of CuS spheres are between 100 nm and 500 nm, which matches well with the SEM analysis. In Fig. 4(c), the diameter of CD was found to be around 4 nm with spherical shape and it shows lattice fringes of 0.23 nm for the d(100) graphitic spacing. Two kinds of lattice fringes are found in HRTEM images and the interplanar distances are approximately 0.31 and 0.35 nm respectively in Fig. 4(d). The interplanar distance of 0.31 nm is representative of the interplanar spacing d(102) of CuS and the interplanar distances of 0.35 nm is assigned to graphitic spacing d(002) of CNTs. The result is in accordance with the XRD analysis of CuS@CD-CNTs composite.

The electrochemical experiments consisting of CV, GCD and EIS were tested to evaluate the electrochemical properties. Fig. 5 (a–c) clearly depicts the CV curves of bare CuS, CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs at different scan rates of 10, 20, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100 mV s−1. The shape of redox waves emerge in all CV curves. This clearly manifests the pseudocapacitive behavior caused by reversible electrochemical reactions and it can be seen commonly in the chalcogenide materials [27,28]. The pseudocapacitive performance of CuS based electrode is obtained by the following reversible redox reaction.
(1) CuS + OH− ↔ CuSOH + e−

Fig. 5(d) presents the comparative CV curves of bare CuS, CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs at 10 mV s−1. Compared to the other two samples, CuS@CD-CNTs electrode possesses larger acreage under the CV curve and it demonstrates higher specific capacitance. The specific capacitance values were calculated from Equation (2),
(2)
C C V =
(
∫
Id V
)
/
( v × m × Δ V )
where CCV (F·g−1) is the specific capacitance calculated from CV curves, I (A) is the charge/discharge current, v (mV·s−1) is the scan rate, m (g) is the mass of active material and ΔV (V) is the potential window. Accordingly, the specific capacitance values of bare CuS, CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs are calculated to 131.7 F g−1, 213.9 F g−1 and 499.8 F g−1 at 10 mV s−1, respectively. The higher specific capacitance of CuS@CD-CNTs is attribute to uniform and excellent intimate contact between CuS spheres and CNTs inside the 3D grape string-like structure due to the addition of CD, simultaneously, accelerates the red-ox reaction [29,30]. The shape of CV curves is almost invariable at different scan rates, which suggest highly reversible features of the CuS@CD-CNTs composite. Fig. 5(e) presents the capacitance retention curves of CuS, CuS-CNTs and CuS@CD-CNTs at various scan rates. It is obviously that CuS@CD-CNTs composite has a better rate performance with 67.3% capacitance retention than that of CuS (27.1%) and CuS-CNTs (55.5%). The superior rate performance of CuS@CD-CNTs can owe to the 3D grape string-like architecture and the tight interfacial contact between CuS and carbon nanotubes. In addition, CD in the composite improved the conductivity of CuS@CD-CNTs and speeded up the transport of ions, enhancing the rate capability. Fig. 5(f) presents the GCD curves of CuS@CD-CNTs at 1 A g−1, 2 A g−1, 3 A g−1, 5A·g−1 and 7A·g−1 and the specific capacitance values are counted to 736.1 F g−1, 626.8 F g−1, 548.2 F g−1, 494.1 F g−1 and 437.7 F g−1, respectively. The specific capacitance values can be counted from the following equation,
(3)
C G C D =
( I × Δ t )
/
( Δ V × m )

CGCD (F·g−1) is the specific capacitance calculated from GCD curves, I (A) is the charge-discharge current, Δt (s) is the discharge time, ΔV (V) is the potential window and m (g) is the mass of active material. The variation of the specific capacitance value of CuS@CD-CNTs is shown in Fig. 6 (a). The specific capacitance acts out a slight decline as a result of the deficient Faradaic redox reaction. To investigate the resistive and capacitive features of CuS@CD-CNTs, EIS measurements were tested at an open circuit potential in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz–100 kHz. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the Nyquist plot is composed of two parts: one is the diameter of the semicircle representing the charge transfer resistance (Rct) at high frequency and the other is the sloped portion of linear part standing for the diffusion resistance at low frequency. In the high frequencies, RΩ located in the left of Fig. 6 (b) represents the internal resistance, including the intrinsic resistance of electrode and the ionic resistance of the electrolyte. The CuS@CD-CNTs (0.65 Ω) has lower internal resistance than CuS (0.78 Ω) and CuS-CNTs (0.71 Ω). The semicircles of CuS@CD-CNTs is not detected at high frequencies, showing significantly low charge transfer resistance. Obviously, CuS@CD-CNTs shows low diffusion resistance (high slope of the linear part) than the pure CuS and CuS-CNTs. This owes to the incorporation of CD between the pure CuS and CNTs, which is beneficial to shorten ion diffusion paths and enhance electrochemical properties. To investigate the stability of CuS@CD-CNTs, cycle performance is performed at 3 A g−1 in 3.0 M KOH electrolyte. Fig. 7 (a) presents the cycle performance with retention of more than 92% after 5000 cycles and the inset shows the charge-discharge curves.

In conclusion, the carbon dot decorated copper sulphide/carbon nanotubes composite with a unique 3D grape string-like structure was synthesized via simple one-step hydrothermal process and characterized by XRD, Raman, SEM and TEM. The CuS@CD-CNTs composite was formed after a reaction time of 12 h at 180 °C and the carbon nanotubes are twining on the side of copper sulfide spheres evenly. Furthermore, the CuS@CD-CNTs composite was served as electrode materials for electrochemical test. The CuS@CD-CNTs electrode behaved encouraging electrochemical performance with a high specific capacitance of 736.1 F g−1 at the current density of 1 A g−1 and long cycling life with 92% retention over 5000 cycles. This improved electrochemical performance owes to the 3D grape string-like structure and the synergy between CuS and CNTs, which shorten the ion diffusion pathway, increasing additional pseudocapacitance. It is expected that the CuS@CD-CNTs will be extensively utilized as efficient and stable electrode material in high-performance supercapacitor applications. Therefore, this work successfully presents a new 3D grape string-like structure between CuS and CNTs and provides a new insight in CuS and carbon based supercapacitor.
The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.
We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

Tingkai Zhao, Tiehu Li, Ishaq Ahmad and Xiarong Peng conceived and designed the study. Xiarong Peng, Xin Zhao, Jingtian Hu, Tao Jiang, Xiaofeng Lu and Heng Zhang performed the experiments. Xiarong Peng and Xin Zhao wrote the paper. Xiarong Peng, Xin Zhao, Jingtian Hu, Tao Jiang, Xiaofeng Lu and Heng Zhang reviewed and edited the manuscript. All authors read and approved the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (51672221, 51872231), the Key Industrial Chain Project of Shaanxi Province (2018ZDCXL-GY-08-07), the Seed Foundation of Innovation and Creation for Graduate Students in Northwestern Polytechnical University (ZZ2018006) and National College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (201810699094). We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Northwestern Polytechnical University for SEM and TEM testing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2019.153057.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flexible electrodes and supercapacitors for wearable energy storage: a review by category

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Dong, C. Xu, L. Yang, Z.H. Huang, F. Kang, Q.H. Yang, Z. Xin, Flexible electrodes and supercapacitors for wearable energy storage: a review by category, J. Mater. Chem. A 4 (2016) 4659-4685.

Study of pseudocapacitive contribution to superior energy storage of 3D heterostructure CoWO4/Co3O4 nanocone arrays, J. Power Sources 418 (2019) 202-210.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flexible full-solid state supercapacitors based on zinc sulfide spheres growing on carbon textile with superior charge storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.S. Javed, C. Jie, C. Lin, X. Yi, C. Zhang, B. Wan, C. Hu, Flexible full-solid state supercapacitors based on zinc sulfide spheres growing on carbon textile with superior charge storage, J. Mater. Chem. A 4 (2015) 667-674.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Morphology controlled synthesis of monodispersed manganese sulfide nanocrystals and their primary application in supercapacitors with high performances

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Tang, T. Chen, S. Yu, Morphology controlled synthesis of monodispersed manganese sulfide nanocrystals and their primary application in supercapacitors with high performances, Chem. Commun. 51 (2015) 9018-9021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Different distribution of in-situ thin carbon layer in hollow cobalt sulfide nanocages and their application for supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Jin, S.Y. Lu, L. Ma, M.Y. Gan, Y. Lei, X.L. Zhang, G. Fu, P.S. Yang, M.F. Yan, Different distribution of in-situ thin carbon layer in hollow cobalt sulfide nanocages and their application for supercapacitors, J. Power Sources 341 (2017) 294-301.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis of layered molybdenum sulfide/N-doped graphene hybrid with enhanced supercapacitor performance

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Controlled synthesis of nickel sulfide/graphene oxide nanocomposite for high-performance supercapacitor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Wang, H. Wang, S. Zhang, C. Mao, J. Song, H. Niu, B. Jin, Y. Tian, Controlled synthesis of nickel sulfide/graphene oxide nanocomposite for high-performance supercapacitor, Appl. Surf. Sci. 282 (2013) 704-708.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hierarchical MnO2 nanosheets on electrospun NiCo2O4 nanotubes as electrode materials for high rate capability and excellent cycling stability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Xu, S. Li, J. Yang, H. Xu, J. Hu, Hierarchical MnO2 nanosheets on electrospun NiCo2O4 nanotubes as electrode materials for high rate capability and excellent cycling stability, J. Alloys Comp. 678 (2016) 120-125.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Double-shell CuS nanocages as advanced supercapacitor electrode materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Guo, X. Zhang, Y. Sun, X. Zhang, T. Lin, Z. Xiao, Double-shell CuS nanocages as advanced supercapacitor electrode materials, J. Power Sources 355 (2017) 31-35.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step solvothermal synthesis of different morphologies CuS nanosheets compared as supercapacitor electrode materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.J. Huang, J.Z. Zhang, Y. Fan, One-step solvothermal synthesis of different morphologies CuS nanosheets compared as supercapacitor electrode materials, J. Alloys Comp. 625 (2015) 158-163.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and properties of morphology controllable copper sulphide nanosheets for supercapacitor application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Xu, Y. Liang, Y. Su, S. Zhu, Z. Cui, X. Yang, A. Inoue, W. Qiang, C. Liang, Synthesis and properties of morphology controllable copper sulphide nanosheets for supercapacitor application, Electrochim. Acta 211 (2016) 891-899.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel solvothermal synthesis of Mn3O4/graphene composites for supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wu, S. Liu, H. Wang, X. Wang, X. Zhang, G. Jin, A novel solvothermal synthesis of Mn3O4/graphene composites for supercapacitors, Electrochim. Acta 90 (2013) 210-218.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile preparation of reduced graphene oxide/copper sulfide composite as electrode materials for supercapacitors with high energy density

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Zhao, W. Yang, Z. Xin, X. Peng, J. Hu, T. Chen, T. Li, Facile preparation of reduced graphene oxide/copper sulfide composite as electrode materials for supercapacitors with high energy density, Compos. Part B Eng. 150 (2018) 60-67.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbons and electrolytes for advanced supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. FrancOis, P. Volker, B. Andrea, F. Elzbieta, Carbons and electrolytes for advanced supercapacitors, Adv. Mater. 26 (2014) 2219-2251.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An overview of the applications of graphene-based materials in supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Huang, J. Liang, Y. Chen, An overview of the applications of graphene-based materials in supercapacitors, Small 8 (2012) 1805-1834.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene-based supercapacitor with an ultrahigh energy density

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Lv, X. Gao, Q. Xu, H. Liu, Y.G. Wang, Y. Xia, Carbon quantum dot-induced MnO2 nanowire formation and construction of a binder-free flexible membrane with excellent superhydrophilicity and enhanced supercapacitor performance, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 9 (2017) 40394-40403.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design and preparation of a ternary composite of graphene oxide/carbon dots/polypyrrole for supercapacitor application: importance and unique role of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Xiang, J. Wang, L. Jian, W. Ji, C. Hao, B. Hong, Design and preparation of a ternary composite of graphene oxide/carbon dots/polypyrrole for supercapacitor application: Importance and unique role of carbon dots, Carbon 115 (2017) 134-146.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Zhen, H. Dou, Z. Bo, W. Fan, X. Wang, Three-dimensional graphene combined with hierarchical CuS for the design of flexible solid-state supercapacitors, Electrochim. Acta 237 (2017) 109-118.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. De, T. Kuila, N.H. Kim, J.H. Lee, Carbon dot stabilized copper sulphide nanoparticles decorated graphene oxide hydrogel for high performance asymmetric supercapacitor, Carbon 122 (2017) 247-257.
